topic: apartment cooking — trying to make perfect croissants

turns.content: day three of trying to make croissants at home and I want to throw my rolling pin out the window
Day three! That's commitment. What's going wrong — is it the lamination, the proofing, or something else entirely?

the lamination is killing me. I understand it intellectually — fold the butter block into the détrempe, do your turns, keep everything cold. I've watched it done a thousand times. but my apartment is warm, my counter is small, and the butter keeps breaking through the dough on the third turn. every single time

Butter breaking through is almost always a temperature problem. If the butter is too cold it shatters through the dough, and if it's too warm it melts and absorbs. You need that sweet spot where it's pliable but still firm. How long are you resting the dough between turns?

thirty minutes in the fridge between each turn. but I think the problem is the rolling itself — my counter is so short that I have to roll in bursts. roll, rotate, roll, and by the time I've wrestled it into the right dimensions the butter has warmed up from my hands and the room. putain, in a pastry kitchen they have marble counters and walk-in coolers. I have a cutting board and a fridge from 2003

The counter length is a real constraint for lamination. Here's a trick — chill the rolling pin in the freezer for ten minutes before each turn. It keeps the dough cooler while you work. And if the room is warm, you can put a bag of ice on the counter surface before you start rolling to bring the temperature down. Dry it off, flour it, and go. That buys you an extra few minutes before the butter softens.

ice on the counter... that's actually clever. I've been trying to fight the temperature by working faster but speed doesn't help when you're cramped. you just end up being rough with the dough and then the layers are uneven. I need to slow down and keep things cold. merde, I know this in theory. I tell junior cooks this at work all the time. "respect the dough." and here I am disrespecting it in my own kitchen like a hypocrite

It's always different when you're the one fighting the conditions. At work you have the environment working for you. At home you have to create that environment from nothing. What about the butter block itself — are you using a European-style high-fat butter?

oui, 84% fat. I'm not using grocery store butter for croissants, that would be criminal. I'm using the same stuff we use at the restaurant. the butter is not the problem, I am the problem. or my kitchen is the problem. or both

You and the kitchen are both factors, but the butter quality is clearly solid. Have you considered doing the turns over two days? First and second turn on day one, rest overnight in the fridge, third turn and shaping on day two. The overnight rest relaxes the gluten and keeps everything cold. It also means you're not fighting the room temperature for as long in a single session.

hm, I've been trying to power through all the turns in one session because that's how we do it at work — but at work we have a sheeter and climate control. maybe splitting it is the move for a home kitchen. I'm going to try that tomorrow. fresh start, two turns, then into the fridge overnight. and I'm doing the ice trick on the counter

That's a solid plan. One more thing — when you shape them, make sure the triangles are cut with a sharp knife or a pizza wheel, not torn. Clean edges help the layers puff evenly. And don't stretch the dough when you roll them up. Gentle tension, not force.

I know I know, I'm a professional cook not a barbarian haha. but honestly the shaping is the part I'm least worried about. if I can get clean layers from the lamination the rest is just technique and time. the proofing is tricky too — my apartment runs warm so they overproof fast if I'm not watching. last batch looked like deflated sad balloons

For the proofing in a warm apartment, try a cooler spot — even a closet or the bathroom if it's cooler than the kitchen. You want them to roughly double in size, and when you gently poke one it should slowly spring back but not all the way. If it springs back fast, underproofed. If it doesn't spring back at all, you've gone too far.

the bathroom. imagine. Babushka would die if she saw me proofing croissants next to the bathtub. actually she'd probably say "just buy them from the bakery like a normal person." she was very practical. okay, attempt number four starts tomorrow. I refuse to be defeated by flour and butter. this is a matter of professional pride now. bonne nuit

Professional pride is a powerful motivator. Two-day method, ice on the counter, chilled rolling pin, and keep the room cool. Attempt four is going to be the one. Bonne nuit — go rest those arms, you've got lamination to do tomorrow.
